serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Mee Goreng Pattaya as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre or Indian Muslim stall, weighing approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Goreng (Indian Style) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists Mee Goreng (Indian style) as a hawker dish. Mee Goreng Pattaya is a variant in which the finished stir-fried noodles are wrapped in a thin omelette; the macronutrient profile per 100 g is closely comparable to standard Indian-style Mee Goreng. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo Mee Goreng (Indian) entry and cross-checked against ICMR-NIN data for similar stir-fried noodle preparations. The egg wrap adds a modest increment to protein and fat; values reflect the composite dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of yellow wheat noodles and stir-fried noodle dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mee Goreng Pattaya. GI estimated at 52 based on yellow wheat noodles (GI ~52–55, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care 44(Suppl 1):S1–S13) as the dominant carbohydrate source. Stir-frying with oil, egg, vegetables, and chilli sauce lowers the effective GI relative to plain boiled noodles. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (52 × 69.3 g) / 100 ≈ 36.0, rounded to 36.2 using 350 g serving × 19.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 69.3 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per standard 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Mee Goreng Pattaya to occasional consumption and request reduced soy sauce and ketchup at point of preparation, as a single serving can contribute approximately 64% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~36 per serving) from yellow wheat noodles; mitigated by egg, fibre, and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Mee Goreng Pattaya in controlled portions (ideally half a standard plate) and pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glycemic response, given the moderate glycemic load of approximately 36 per full serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content; saturated fat relatively low at ~1.1 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Mee Goreng Pattaya poses a moderate risk primarily due to its high sodium content per serving; the saturated fat level is relatively modest, but frequent consumption should be avoided and preparation with less oil and sauce is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Goreng Pattaya is a popular Singaporean Indian-Muslim hawker dish consisting of stir-fried yellow wheat noodles cooked in a wok with egg, potato cubes, tofu, bean sprouts, tomato, fresh chilli, curry powder, tomato ketchup, and soy sauce. The defining characteristic of the Pattaya variant is that the finished stir-fried noodles are encased in a thin folded omelette, creating a parcel presentation that distinguishes it from standard Mee Goreng. The dish has a bold, tangy, mildly spicy flavour profile with a soft noodle texture and a slightly crisp egg exterior. It is commonly served at Indian Muslim hawker stalls and kopitiams across Singapore, and is closely associated with the broader Mee Goreng tradition brought to Singapore by Tamil Muslim and Jawi Peranakan communities. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 20 g protein, 18 g fat, and 69 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high at approximately 1470 mg per serving, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low GI) based on yellow wheat noodles as the primary carbohydrate source, though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 36 due to the large noodle portion. The egg wrap contributes additional protein and cholesterol. The dish is generally halal and dairy-free but contains gluten (wheat noodles), egg, and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for Mee Goreng Pattaya exists in HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN; values are adapted from the closest HPB Foodo entry for Indian-style Mee Goreng with adjustment for the egg wrap component. GI is an analogous estimate based on yellow wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo Mee Goreng (Indian style) and cross-checked against ICMR-NIN stir-fried noodle data; the egg omelette wrap is accounted for in the protein and fat estimates. 2. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 4. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in vegetable-oil stir-fried preparations per HPB source. 9. GI set to 52 as an analogous estimate based on yellow wheat noodles (University of Sydney GI Database); gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule. 10. Serving size of 350 g is based on typical hawker-centre plate weight for this dish category. 11. The Pattaya variant name does not refer to the Thai city but is a colloquial Singaporean hawker term for the omelette-wrapped presentation style.
